meta.title: X290 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes X290 engine — M256 straight-six, M177 V8, and the 843bhp plug-in hybrid. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and owner verdicts. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-AMG GT X290 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X290 engine typically costs £3,000–£15,000 supply only, depending on engine — the M256 straight-six sits at £3,000–£5,000, while the M177 V8 ranges from £6,000–£14,000. The hybrid M177.880 tops the range at £9,000–£15,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,300.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes-AMG GT X290 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M177.980 V8 in the GT 63 (585 bhp) is our top recommendation — it's well-understood, has a documented failure pattern (mostly oil leaks, not catastrophic), and avoids the 48V complexity of the M256 and the hybrid system of the M177.880. The GT 63 S shares the same core engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-AMG GT X290?

acceptedAnswer.text: Valve cover oil leaks on the M177 V8 (hot‑vee heat accelerates gasket wear), 48V starter‑generator and auxiliary battery failures on the M256 straight‑six, and — on the GT 63 S E Performance — hybrid system complexity that brings twice the powertrain components and twice the potential failure points.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-AMG GT X290 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, if you choose carefully and maintain proactively. The M177 V8 is a robust engine with well‑documented, manageable issues. The M256's 48V system is the biggest question mark on early cars. The plug‑in hybrid is reliable for what it is, but its complexity means higher running costs and fewer specialists.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes-AMG GT X290 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is an outright 'avoid', but the GT 63 S E Performance (M177.880) is the one we'd counsel caution on if budget is a concern — its hybrid battery and dual‑powertrain complexity make it the costliest to maintain. For most buyers, the GT 63 with the non‑hybrid M177 is the sweet spot.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my X290 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil leaks that leave puddles or smoke (M177), persistent 48V warning lights or loss of stop‑start function (M256), or a noticeable drop in electric range / hybrid system errors (M177.880) are the key warning signs. Have any of these checked immediately to avoid escalation.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-AMG GT X290 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a well‑kept £40,000+ X290, a £6,000–£10,000 M177 recon is only 15–25% of the car's value — well within our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. Even the £9,000–£15,000 hybrid recon is viable at 20–35%. These are high‑value cars; replacement almost always makes financial sense.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes-AMG GT X290 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With proper servicing, the M177 V8 can comfortably exceed 150,000 miles — oil leaks are its limiting factor, not the bottom end. The M256's 48V system is the longevity question mark; many owners report 80,000+ miles without major issues, but battery replacement at 100k is a realistic budget item. The hybrid's battery will likely need attention before 100,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the X290 and the two-door AMG GT?

acceptedAnswer.text: The X290 (this page) is the five‑door, four‑seat liftback (2018–present), built on the MRA platform shared with the E‑Class. The two‑door AMG GT (C190/R190) is a separate, dedicated sports car platform with a different chassis, different engine mounting, and distinct maintenance costs. Always check which body style you're looking at.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-AMG GT X290 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is stamped on the block — visible from below on the M177 (check behind the front wheel arch liner) and on the M256 near the starter motor. It's also listed on the VIN plate under the bonnet and in the vehicle documentation. Tell us your variant and we'll confirm it.

hero.h1: Mercedes-AMG GT X290 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the AMG GT four-door coupé — from the electrified straight-six M256 to the thunderous M177 V8 that defines AMG's modern era. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the hybrid V8 that rewrote the rulebook. Backed by 370+ real UK M177 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes-AMG GT X290 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X290 four-door coupé — from the 367bhp mild-hybrid straight-six to the 843bhp plug-in hybrid V8.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: M177.980: 90% confidence — 370 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries in 2025; failure patterns documented in Known Failure Patterns table. M256.930: 70% confidence — pattern-data tier; no separate EM enquiry figure exists. M177.880: 70% confidence — pattern-data tier; hybrid system adds complexity.

overview.intro: The X290 is the four-door liftback variant of the Mercedes-AMG GT range, introduced in 2018 as a five-door executive express that shares its MRA platform with the E-Class. It marked AMG's first four-door coupé, combining the brand's racing DNA with genuine practicality — and, from 2021, the most powerful production Mercedes-AMG ever made in the 843bhp GT 63 S E Performance.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-Benz MRA (Modular Rear Architecture) • Predecessor: Mercedes-AMG CLS 63 (C218) • Successor: — (still in production) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (X290 is a standalone four-door model; two-door AMG GT is C190/R190).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The M177 V8 engine generated 370 real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], concentrated on GT 63 and GT 63 S variants as these high-performance models exit warranty.

items.quote: no diesel engine fitted to the X290.

The 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 that defines AMG's modern era — hot-vee layout, responsive delivery, and a soundtrack that justifies the premium. Oil leaks from valve covers and coolant system issues are its known weak points, but the underlying engine is robust.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 theatre without the hybrid system's complexity.

items.quote: The electrified straight-six is clever engineering, but the 48V EQ Boost system adds complexity beyond a conventional engine — starter-generator and auxiliary battery failures are emerging as the primary concerns.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who prioritise efficiency and accept the hybrid system's long-term costs.

items.quote: The plug-in hybrid V8 combines the M177's complexity with a 150 kW electric motor and 4.8 kWh battery. Two powertrains mean two sets of failure points — and the highest replacement cost in the range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the ultimate AMG and have the budget to maintain it.

items.quote: The entry-level V8 X290 offers the same core engine as the GT 63 S, with slightly less power but the same character — and significantly lower acquisition and maintenance costs than the hybrid flagship.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 performance without the hybrid premium.

items.quote: The higher-output straight-six offers genuine performance (4.5s to 60mph) with better fuel economy than any V8 — and avoids the M177's hot-vee heat management entirely. It's the sensible AMG.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want AMG credentials with running costs that won't shock.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Electrified Risk — hybrid system adds cost.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve cover oil leaks, coolant system
Valve cover oil leaks, coolant system
rows.ownershipVerdict: Premium V8 — more power, similar risk.
Most Complex — two powertrains, two risks.

economicsRule.title: The X290 rule
economicsRule.text: A £6,000–£10,000 M177 recon against a £40,000+ X290 sits at 15–25% of the car's value — comfortably within our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. Even the £9,000–£15,000 plug-in hybrid recon is only 20–35% against a £45,000+ example. These are not cheap cars to run, but the economics work — provided you buy well and maintain diligently.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X290 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: GT 53 (M256.930) — better fuel economy, lower parts costs than the V8.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X290 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: GT 63 S E Performance — hybrid V8 complexity and battery replacement risk.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X290 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: GT 63 (M177.980) — V8 character without the hybrid premium.

problems.title: M177 V8 Valve Cover Oil Leaks
problems.affectedModels: X290 GT 63, GT 63 S (all M177.980 variants).
problems.rootCause: The hot-vee layout concentrates heat between the cylinder banks, accelerating gasket and seal degradation — valve cover oil leaks are the most common complaint, with oil seeping onto exhaust manifolds.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of oil smell or visible seepage around the valve covers, have the gaskets replaced immediately — an £800–£1,500 specialist repair is far cheaper than the £8,000+ replacement that follows if oil loss leads to bearing failure.

cta.label: Compare M177 engine prices for your X290 GT 63 →

problems.title: M256 48V EQ Boost System Failures
problems.rootCause: The integrated starter-generator (ISG) and 48V auxiliary battery are stressed by the system's frequent start-stop and boost operations — failures lead to warning lights, reduced power, and in some cases, complete system shutdown.
problems.recommendation: The 48V system is the defining risk of the M256 — a £800–£1,500 preventative battery replacement at 60k miles is cheap insurance against a £3,000–£5,000 full engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare M256 engine prices for your X290 GT 53 →

problems.title: M177.880 Plug-in Hybrid System Complexity
problems.rootCause: Two powertrains — a 4.0-litre V8 at the front and a 150 kW electric motor at the rear — mean twice the systems to fail. The 4.8 kWh battery is the single most expensive component; cooling system integration between combustion and electric drivetrains adds further failure points.
problems.recommendation: The GT 63 S E Performance is the most complex AMG ever built — if you buy one, budget for specialist inspection of both powertrains before purchase, and accept that a full replacement could exceed the value of lesser cars entirely.

problems.title: AMG Speedshift MCT 9G Transmission Wear
problems.rootCause: The wet-clutch multi-clutch transmission is stressed by the torque from both V8 and hybrid powertrains — clutch wear and mechatronic unit failures are emerging as common high-mileage issues.
problems.recommendation: On any X290 over 50,000 miles, treat the gearbox service as non-negotiable — an £800 fluid change is trivial against a £4,000+ replacement.

replacementCosts.h2: X290 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the baseline figures — the X290 four-door shares its MRA platform with the E-Class, and engine-bay access is consistent across the range. M177 costs are inherited from the EM data's M177 line (£6,000–£10,000 reconditioned); M256 costs are estimated from the broader premium V6/straight-six replacement market.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are X290 four-door liftback only. The two-door AMG GT (C190/R190) is a separate model with different chassis and should not be confused with this generation.

mostRequestedEngines: 3. M177.880 (GT 63 S E Performance) — pattern data
